metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving approach for 'no other principal is granted execute-api:Invoke' in AC1 — e.g., a `conftest`/OPA rule or a `grep -rE 'execute-api:Invoke' infra/terraform/ | assert only rebar-opcert-admin` script step that exhaustively scans all Terraform IAM policy documents and fails if any resource other than the `rebar-opcert-admin` inline policy contains this permission.
findings.finding: AC1 asserts 'no other principal is granted [execute-api:Invoke]' but provides no proving command or test approach for this negative assertion. Asserting an absence across all IAM policy documents in the Terraform configuration (inline policies, resource-based policies, any future additions) requires exhaustive scanning, not just verifying the one IaC-created role. With no described approach, an executor has no guidance on how to implement this check, and a test that only verifies the `rebar-opcert-admin` role's inline policy exists would silently pass even if a second policy inadvertently also granted Invoke.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'A conftest (or equivalent) policy test scans all `aws_iam_role_policy`, `aws_iam_policy_document`, and `aws_iam_role` resources in `infra/terraform/` and asserts that `execute-api:Invoke` appears only in the `rebar-opcert-admin` inline policy; the test fails if any other resource document contains this action.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the materialize script location: either place `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` in `infra/gerrit/` to match the cited precedents, or explicitly document that `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` must be updated to call the new script (and include that update in scope).
findings.finding: The plan cites the materialize-script precedent as 'infra/gerrit/materialize-deploy-key.sh / materialize-g2p-config.sh' (both confirmed in infra/gerrit/), yet places the new script at infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh — a different directory. The plan calls this 'SSM → file, same pattern,' but infra/scripts/ contains no existing materialize scripts (only compose-up.sh, fetch-secrets.sh, etc.). This means the 'same path pattern' assertion is false, and compose-up.sh (which hard-codes the paths of the other two scripts) will not pick up the new script automatically.
findings.location: What section — 'the new script is infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh … SSM → file, same pattern; infra/gerrit/materialize-deploy-key.sh / materialize-g2p-config.sh'
findings.suggested_fix: Move the script to infra/gerrit/materialize-opcert-guard.sh (matching the precedent directory) and add a call in infra/scripts/compose-up.sh analogous to the existing materialize-g2p-config.sh invocation at line 177.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` to the plan's edit-target list and specify a call to `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` before `docker compose up`, following the `materialize-g2p-config.sh` precedent (including its non-fatal / fail-closed policy decision).
findings.finding: `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` is an unenumerated consumer: every existing materialize script (`materialize-deploy-key.sh`, `materialize-g2p-config.sh`) is explicitly called from `compose-up.sh` before `docker compose up` (lines 177, 127). The new `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` follows that precedent, but the plan never enumerates `compose-up.sh` as a file requiring a call-site addition — leaving the guard value unmaterialized on every fresh boot.
findings.location: What section — Compose subsection; `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` lines 172-183
findings.suggested_fix: In the Compose subsection, add: 'extend `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` to call `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` before `docker compose up -d --build`, following the `materialize-g2p-config.sh` precedent (non-fatal: a missing guard must not block the whole stack from booting, but must emit a loud warning).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update AC4's `trusted_environments.yaml` pinning to use `added_at_log_position` (a tickets-branch log position stamped by the `rebar trusted-env` helper) instead of `added_at_commit` (a main-branch SHA), matching the schema the parent's security finding #5 supersedes the earlier protocol with.
findings.finding: The leaf's AC4 specifies pinning the deployed public key in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` 'with `added_at_commit` per the epic's era protocol', but the parent's later 'Authoritative era anchor (security finding #5)' section explicitly SUPERSEDES the earlier 'Trust config & era-validity protocol' and renames the field to `added_at_log_position` (a tickets-branch log position, not a main-branch SHA). The leaf uses the old, superseded field name, contradicting the parent's current authoritative schema.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC4 / `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` pinning
findings.suggested_fix: In AC4, replace 'with `added_at_commit` per the epic's era protocol' with 'with `added_at_log_position` (the current tickets-branch tip, operator-stamped via the `rebar trusted-env` helper) per the epic's era protocol (finding #5 in the epic's "Authoritative era anchor" section)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in AC3 how the materialize-script rendering assertion is implemented: state whether the test (a) performs a config/content-only inspection of the script (asserting the correct SSM path and rendering pattern), (b) mocks AWS SSM for a unit-level render test, or (c) is an integration test requiring live account credentials — and add a stated default so an executor can act without stopping to ask.
findings.finding: AC3 asserts that 'a test asserts... the materialize script renders the guard value from its SSM parameter,' but rendering requires either live SSM access (account-bound, not in-session for a unit test) or a mock/stub strategy. The plan specifies neither; an executor writing a unit test has no guidance on whether to mock SSM, perform a config-content-only inspection, or treat this as an integration test requiring live credentials — leaving a real risk that the assertion is implemented as a weaker script-existence check that does not actually verify rendering behavior.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify what happens when the `random_password`-generated guard value is replaced: add a procedure note (in the deploy section or a runbook pointer) that `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` must be re-run on the box and nginx reloaded whenever the SSM parameter `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard` changes, to keep the nginx-enforced value in sync with the API Gateway static header.
findings.finding: If the `random_password` resource generating the origin-guard value is ever replaced (state taint, state loss, provider upgrade, or explicit `terraform taint`), Terraform will update the SSM parameter and the API Gateway static `X-Opcert-Guard` request-header mapping to the new value atomically from its perspective — but nginx on the box will still be enforcing the OLD guard value until `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` is re-run. During that window every request through API Gateway will present the new guard and nginx will reject it, silently breaking the service. The plan specifies no re-materialization trigger or procedure for this scenario.
findings.location: What section — 'the origin-guard header provisioning path' + Compose/nginx wiring; AC3 (materialize script)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence in the 'Deploy + prove' section: 'If the origin-guard `random_password` is ever replaced (e.g. state taint or rotation), re-run `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` on the box and reload nginx before treating API Gateway rejections as the expected unauthorized-request check.' Optionally, have the materialize script be idempotent and called from `compose-up.sh` on every deploy (like `materialize-g2p-config.sh`), so the value is always refreshed on a normal redeploy.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief runbook note (or Terraform `check` block / `output`) describing how to safely rotate the origin guard (taint random_password → apply → update SSM param value → reload nginx) and explicitly state that `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` must be removed / temporarily overridden during intentional rotation.
findings.finding: The origin-guard value is generated by `random_password` in Terraform state and simultaneously stored in SSM SecureString with `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }`. If the random_password resource is ever tainted or recreated (e.g., `terraform taint`, module refactor, or state surgery), the API Gateway integration header silently updates to the new value while the SSM parameter retains the old one, breaking the origin guard without any error until requests start failing.
findings.location: What section — Terraform bullet, origin-guard provisioning paragraph

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the API Gateway HTTP_PROXY integration URI scheme explicitly as `https://` (port 443) in the plan, and note that the existing Let's Encrypt certificate on the box satisfies TLS verification — the current :80 nginx config returns a 301 redirect that API Gateway HTTP_PROXY does not follow.
findings.finding: The API Gateway HTTP_PROXY integration URI scheme (http:// vs https://) is unspecified, but the existing nginx :80 config returns a 301 redirect for non-ACME traffic — API Gateway HTTP API HTTP_PROXY integrations do NOT follow redirects, so an http:// integration target would propagate the 301 to callers rather than proxying the service response.
findings.location: What section — 'an API Gateway HTTP API … HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx origin for the /opcert/ routes'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, change 'HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx origin' to 'HTTPS-integrated to the box's nginx origin at https://rebar.solutions.navateam.com/opcert/ (the Let's Encrypt cert on the box satisfies TLS verification from APIGW; using http:// on port 80 would cause API Gateway to receive and forward a 301 redirect rather than proxy the service response).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the backend URI scheme for the API Gateway → EC2 integration leg: if http:// (acceptable for PoC given the single-tenant context and reduced blast radius vs. the guard being a secondary control), state this explicitly with rationale; if https://, confirm the nginx listener on the origin and specify how the TLS certificate is presented to API Gateway.
findings.finding: The API Gateway → EC2 origin integration leg's protocol (http:// vs https://) is undeclared. The plan says 'HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx origin' but never states the backend URI scheme. Because the plan explicitly rejects a VPC Link (which would keep traffic inside AWS's private network), the integration target is the box's public IP/DNS in a default VPC with only public subnets — meaning plaintext http:// would send the origin-guard secret value and signed job payloads over the public internet between API Gateway and the EC2 host.
findings.location: What section — 'an API Gateway HTTP API (v2) … HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx origin' / AC1 — 'the box-origin integration with the static X-Opcert-Guard request-header mapping'
findings.suggested_fix: The simplest sound option for this single-tenant PoC: configure the integration target as `https://rebar.solutions.navateam.com/opcert/` (the existing Let's Encrypt-terminated public hostname) rather than a bare IP/port. API Gateway will then TLS-verify against the existing cert, keeping the guard header confidential in transit with no new infrastructure. Add one sentence to the What section: 'The integration backend URI is https://rebar.solutions.navateam.com/opcert/ (TLS via the existing Let's Encrypt certificate) so the origin-guard header is confidential in transit.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace `hashicorp/random` + `random_password` for the origin-guard with the codebase-idiomatic approach: declare `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard` as an SSM SecureString placeholder with `value = "CHANGEME"` + `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` (matching the pattern in `infra/terraform/ssm.tf` for all 12 existing secrets), seed the real value out-of-band via `openssl rand -hex 32 | aws ssm put-parameter ...`, and read it from SSM in the materialize script — eliminating the new provider dependency entirely.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `hashicorp/random` (a new Terraform provider) and `random_password` solely to generate the origin-guard value — a single use-site — while the codebase's established pattern for all 12 existing SSM SecureString secrets is: declare with placeholder `"CHANGEME"` + `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }`, then seed out-of-band via `aws ssm put-parameter`. Zero existing `random_password` uses exist in `infra/terraform/`. This violates Rule-of-Three (0 existing call-sites) and is a cargo-cult import of a provider whose only value here is replicated by the already-idiomatic one-liner `openssl rand -hex 32 | aws ssm put-parameter ...` — which also avoids persisting the guard secret in Terraform remote state.
findings.location: What section — Terraform bullet: 'the `hashicorp/random` provider is ADDED to `infra/terraform/versions.tf` `required_providers`'; AC1: 'the `hashicorp/random` provider in `required_providers`'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Terraform declaration for `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard`, use `value = "CHANGEME"` + `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` (exactly as `aws_ssm_parameter.rebar_secrets` does for the 12 existing secrets). In the deploy procedure, add: `guard=$(openssl rand -hex 32) && aws ssm put-parameter --name /rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard --type SecureString --value "$guard" --overwrite`. Remove `hashicorp/random` from `required_providers` and `versions.tf`. The materialize script reads the value from SSM at boot (the existing pattern), and the API Gateway integration reads it from the SSM parameter via a `aws_ssm_parameter` data source at plan time.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the `hashicorp/random` / Terraform-owned origin-guard approach explicitly against the established operator-seed alternative (e.g., `openssl rand` + `aws ssm put-parameter`), and address whether storing the guard value in Terraform state is acceptable given the existing CI drift-detection role's state read access.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `hashicorp/random` (a new Terraform provider) with a single use-site — `random_password` for the origin-guard value — while every one of the codebase's 12+ existing SSM SecureString secrets follows a strictly different pattern: operator-seeded out-of-band, with `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` so Terraform never owns the secret value. The plan does not compare against the established operator-seed pattern for the origin-guard (e.g., `openssl rand -hex 32` + `aws ssm put-parameter`, same as the ed25519 key seeding step already in scope). The chosen approach stores the guard value in Terraform state (even encrypted), which is a novel departure from codebase precedent that the plan justifies only implicitly ('Terraform generates the guard value … closing the loop') without acknowledging the state-storage exposure or the alternative.
findings.location: What section: 'the origin-guard header provisioning path: Terraform generates the guard value with `random_password`' and 'the `hashicorp/random` provider is ADDED to `infra/terraform/versions.tf`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add observability requirements: at minimum, specify that nginx logs guard-header rejection events at WARN level, the materialize script logs success/failure to syslog or a named log file, and a CloudWatch metric alarm covers API Gateway 4xx rate for the opcert API.
findings.finding: The plan introduces two new failure surfaces (API Gateway unreachable, SSM unavailable at service startup) but specifies no observability instrumentation — no metrics, logs, or alerts — for guard-header rejections at nginx, materialize script failures, or API Gateway 4xx/5xx rates, leaving operators unable to detect or debug authentication failures in production.
findings.location: What section — 'Deploy + prove'; AC item 5 (operator-attested live checks)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the ADR 0046 L4 citation: L4 addresses threshold/multi-party signing (not required), not key-storage backend; the actual justification for SSM over Secrets Manager/KMS is cost (~$50+/mo new spend), and ADR 0046 L4 in fact anticipated KMS/secrets manager — remove the false 'L4 does not require isolation' framing and state the real (cost) rationale directly.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'isolation ADR 0046 L4 does not require' as the authoritative justification for rejecting KMS/Secrets Manager. The actual text of ADR 0046 L4 states: 'a controlled environment with least-privilege key custody (KMS/secrets manager, per the op-cert plan) meets the bar' — i.e., L4 is about threshold/multi-party signing (which is not required), not about whether KMS or SSM Parameter Store is used for key custody. L4 in fact explicitly anticipated KMS/Secrets Manager as the key-custody implementation ('per the op-cert plan'). The plan's citation misrepresents L4's scope and uses it to justify an architectural choice (SSM over Secrets Manager/KMS) that L4 does not address.
findings.location: Why section — 'A dedicated Fargate/NLB/Secrets-Manager/KMS-CMK stack was rejected (~$50+/mo new spend for isolation ADR 0046 L4 does not require)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'isolation ADR 0046 L4 does not require' with: 'the cost-justified choice — ADR 0046 L4's requirement (single controlled environment with least-privilege key custody) is satisfied by SSM SecureString, which avoids the ~$50+/mo Secrets Manager + KMS-CMK fixed fee while meeting the same bar.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` as a consumer requiring a `BOT_PATHS` extension (or equivalent service-scoped trigger) for `infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert` so opcert image changes auto-deploy on the live box.
findings.finding: `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh`'s `BOT_PATHS` variable (line 55) determines which file changes trigger an automated opcert service rebuild on the live box. The plan adds `infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert` (from the sibling story) as a new Dockerfile that affects the opcert service, but `autodeploy.sh` is not enumerated as a consumer requiring a `BOT_PATHS` extension for `infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert`.
findings.location: `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` line 55; plan's Compose subsection

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge in the plan that `random_password` stores the origin-guard value in Terraform state (S3, encrypted), and confirm that the existing state-bucket access control (and the GHA `rebar-terraform-plan` role's ReadOnlyAccess) is an acceptable read path for this secret — or switch to OOB guard seeding (matching the ed25519 key pattern) to keep the guard out of state.
findings.finding: The `random_password` resource for the origin-guard value causes that value to be stored as plaintext in Terraform state (the S3 backend, `rebar/prod/terraform.tfstate`). The plan states the two SSM SecureString parameters are the sensitive stores and asserts 'NO new/broader IAM grant', but it does not disclose that the guard value is also readable by any principal with S3 `GetObject` + KMS `Decrypt` access to the state bucket — which is a distinct (if encrypted) read path not covered by the `/rebar/prod/*` SSM grant. The plan's security reasoning is incomplete in not acknowledging this.
findings.location: What section — 'the origin-guard header provisioning path'; 'NO new fixed-monthly-cost resources'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the assertion that HTTP API v2 has no resource policy (it does, since 2022); explicitly state whether a resource policy was considered as defense-in-depth and, if not added, why (e.g. the single IaC-created role + identity policy is judged sufficient at the FISMA Moderate target per ADR 0046 L4).
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' as the justification for using only an identity/inline policy on the IaC-created caller role. This is an unverified capability claim that is factually incorrect: AWS API Gateway HTTP APIs have supported resource-based policies since their 2022 GA update, documented under 'Controlling access to HTTP APIs with AWS IAM'. The error is architecturally conservative (the plan builds a stricter model), but mischaracterizes the service's capabilities, may cause confusion when writing the Terraform, and forecloses a defense-in-depth option (a resource policy could additionally pin the allowed source role, giving two independent enforcement points instead of one).
findings.location: What section: 'Caller restriction is identity-policy-based (an HTTP API has no resource policy)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' with an accurate statement such as: 'HTTP APIs do support resource policies, but the plan relies solely on the identity policy on the IaC-created `rebar-opcert-admin` role, judged sufficient at the FISMA Moderate target; a resource policy could be added as defense-in-depth in a higher-adversary scenario (deferred per ADR 0046 L4).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add API Gateway access logging (aws_apigatewayv2_stage with access_log_settings pointing to a CloudWatch log group) and at least one CloudWatch alarm (e.g., on 4xx or 5xx count) for the opcert API Gateway, consistent with the existing monitoring.tf pattern.
findings.finding: No observability (API Gateway access logs, CloudWatch alarms, or metrics) is specified for the new API Gateway HTTP API or the opcert service, leaving the new internet-facing endpoint without any ongoing health signal, error detection, or audit trail.
findings.location: What section — Terraform bullet / AC1 / AC5; no monitoring.tf extension mentioned
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the new Terraform file: an `aws_cloudwatch_log_group` for API GW access logs (retention_in_days matching existing practice), wire it into the `aws_apigatewayv2_stage` via `access_log_settings { destination_arn = ... }`, and add an `aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm` on `AWS/ApiGateway` metric `5XXError` (or `4XXError` for auth failures) routed to the existing `aws_sns_topic.alerts`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit key-loss recovery / rotation procedure (e.g., re-generate, re-seed SSM, update `trusted_environments.yaml` with a new `env_id` and `added_at_commit`) so the irreversible seeding step has a documented back-out/recovery path as required by T4.
findings.finding: The key-generation and SSM-seeding step is explicitly described as irreversible ('the key exists nowhere else') and is justified as a security property, but the plan provides no rollback or recovery procedure for key-loss scenarios (e.g., accidental SSM parameter deletion, corruption, or account lockout). T4 requires an explicit rollback/back-out plan — acknowledgement of irreversibility alone is insufficient.
findings.location: ## What — SSM Parameter Store SecureString paragraph + AC5 (operator-attested deploy)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Key rotation / recovery' sub-section under 'Deploy + prove': (1) generate a new Ed25519 keypair, (2) `aws ssm put-parameter --overwrite` to replace the SecureString, (3) redeploy the compose service to pick up the new key, (4) append a new entry (new `env_id`, new public key, new `added_at_commit`) to `trusted_environments.yaml`. Note that the old `env_id` entry should be retired. This makes the irreversibility intentional-and-bounded rather than open-ended.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a step (in Scope or What) to create or update an ADR / design-doc entry capturing the two-layer security architecture (SigV4 at API Gateway + origin-guard at nginx), the propagation path of the guard value, and the rationale for each layer — so the contract is not tribal knowledge.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel two-layer security architecture (SigV4 at API Gateway + origin-guard header at nginx) as the trusted-gate contract, but does not declare creation of a new ADR or design-doc update capturing this pattern. Existing referenced ADRs (0046, 0047) document the cost/isolation REJECTION decisions, not the resulting architecture. Future maintainers changing the nginx config, rotating the guard, or extending the API Gateway integration have no canonical reference explaining WHY the two layers exist and how they interlock.
findings.location: What section (API Gateway + nginx origin-guard description); Acceptance Criteria AC-1, AC-3
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope 'In' list: 'ADR XXXX (or an update to the Producer-signing design resolution doc) recording the opcert security architecture: SigV4 enforcement at API Gateway layer, origin-guard header injection by API Gateway and validation by nginx, the SSM-backed rotation path, and why both layers are needed.' Add a corresponding AC: 'An ADR or design-doc section capturing the two-layer security contract exists and is referenced from infra/terraform/ (e.g., a comment with the ADR link).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` uses atomic writes (write to a temp file then `mv`) and retries SSM `get-parameter` with backoff (e.g., up to 3 attempts) before exiting non-zero, and that any non-zero exit aborts the nginx reload.
findings.finding: The materialize script (infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh) reads from SSM Parameter Store and writes to the nginx conf include, but the plan specifies no retry/backoff for transient SSM failures, no atomic write (temp-file + mv) to prevent partial/corrupt nginx conf, and no defined behavior if the script fails mid-write — a partial write could leave nginx with a malformed include, causing nginx reload to fail and taking Gerrit down.
findings.location: What section — 'the new script is infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh'; compose/nginx wiring description
findings.suggested_fix: In the script spec: `aws ssm get-parameter ... || { sleep 2; aws ssm get-parameter ...; } || exit 1; printf '...' > /tmp/opcert-guard.tmp && mv /tmp/opcert-guard.tmp /etc/nginx/conf.d/opcert-guard.conf && nginx -t && nginx -s reload`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit `nginx -t` validation step (and abort on failure) before any `nginx -s reload` in the opcert deploy/materialize procedure, and document this in the AC or deploy steps.
findings.finding: The plan adds a new nginx route for /opcert/ that requires an nginx config reload on the live Gerrit box, but specifies no pre-reload config validation step (`nginx -t`) before the reload is applied — a misconfigured opcert nginx include would cause the reload to fail and could leave nginx in a degraded state, potentially taking Gerrit offline.
findings.location: What section — 'an nginx route /opcert/ → the service'; Deploy + prove section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a key rotation/retirement runbook in the plan: how to overwrite the SSM SecureString manually (bypassing lifecycle ignore_changes), update the public key in trusted_environments.yaml, restart the compose service, and decommission the SSM parameter + yaml entry when the environment is retired.
findings.finding: The Ed25519 private key (`/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`) shared state has a defined CREATE path (out-of-band ssh-keygen + SSM seed) and UPDATE guard (lifecycle ignore_changes), but no RETIRE or ROTATION procedure is defined — there is no specified process for key compromise, expiry, or environment decommission, and the corresponding public key is pinned in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` with no documented removal/rotation path.
findings.location: What section — 'an SSM Parameter Store SecureString /rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key'; '.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml' pinning; Scope section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the leaf's `file_impact` metadata to replace the stale `infra/opcert/` entry (Fargate + Secrets Manager) with the correct paths: `infra/terraform/` (API Gateway HTTP API, IAM role, SSM SecureStrings, random provider) and `infra/compose/` (opcert service, nginx wiring) and `infra/scripts/` (materialize-opcert-guard.sh), matching the parent's resolved design.
findings.finding: The leaf ticket's `file_impact` metadata lists `infra/opcert/` with reason 'Terraform for API GW + Fargate + Secrets Manager' — contradicting both the leaf's own plan body (which correctly places all Terraform in `infra/terraform/` and explicitly rejects Fargate and Secrets Manager) and the parent's resolved design (which also specifies `infra/terraform/` and rejects Fargate/Secrets Manager in the cost-verification comment). The stale metadata diverges from the parent's current resolved scope.
findings.location: Leaf ticket `file_impact` metadata / `infra/opcert/` entry
findings.suggested_fix: Replace file_impact entry `{"path": "infra/opcert/", "reason": "Terraform for API GW + Fargate + Secrets Manager"}` with `{"path": "infra/terraform/", "reason": "API Gateway HTTP API v2, rebar-opcert-admin IAM role, SSM SecureStrings, hashicorp/random provider"}`, `{"path": "infra/compose/", "reason": "opcert service in docker-compose.yml and nginx guarded route"}`, and `{"path": "infra/scripts/", "reason": "materialize-opcert-guard.sh"}`, and `{"path": ".rebar/trusted_environments.yaml", "reason": "pin deployed env public key and env_id"}`.

findings.finding: The SSM SecureString parameter `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` is described as 'declared; value seeded out-of-band,' but the plan specifies no `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` guard or equivalent protection. On any subsequent `terraform apply`, Terraform will attempt to reconcile the parameter value back to whatever the resource declaration specifies — silently overwriting or nulling the seeded private key, which 'exists nowhere else.' This is an irreversible key loss with no rollback path.
findings.location: What section — Terraform subsection, SSM parameter `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`; Acceptance Criteria bullet 1
findings.suggested_fix: In the Terraform resource for the key parameter, add `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` so Terraform tracks the resource's existence but never overwrites the externally-seeded secret value. Document this as the explicit, justified choice for out-of-band secret seeding.

findings.finding: The plan does not specify `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` on the two new SSM SecureString parameters (`/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`, `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard`). Without it, a subsequent `terraform apply` would overwrite the operator-seeded Ed25519 private key and the materialized origin-guard with Terraform's placeholder value, breaking the deployed service.
findings.location: What section — SSM Parameter Store SecureString declarations; AC line 1 ('the two SecureString parameters … under the existing rebar-gerrit-ssm-params-read path grant')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to both new `aws_ssm_parameter` blocks: `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` — identical to the existing `aws_ssm_parameter.rebar_secrets` resource in ssm.tf. Document in the AC that this block is required so the test assertion can verify it.

findings.finding: ACs 1, 3, and 4 each say 'a test asserts …' without naming the test file, runner, or invocation command. An executor knows WHAT to assert but has no concrete proving command to run in-session to produce evidence of passage — violating E6's requirement that every completion-relevant claim has a concrete proving command that produces evidence on success.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 ('A test asserts these declarations exist'), AC3 ('a test asserts the service entry…'), AC4 ('a test asserts the entry parses…')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single line to each AC naming the test file and runner: e.g. AC1: 'Verified by `pytest infra/tests/test_opcert_iac.py -v` (covers the three sub-assertions)'; similarly for AC3 (`pytest infra/tests/test_opcert_compose.py -v`) and AC4 (`pytest infra/tests/test_trusted_envs.py -v`).

findings.finding: The plan describes 'an API Gateway HTTP API with an IAM (SigV4) authorizer' using REST API (v1) terminology. For an API Gateway HTTP API (v2 / APIGatewayV2), AWS_IAM authentication is not implemented with a separate `aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer` resource — HTTP API authorizers support only `JWT` and `REQUEST` types. AWS_IAM for HTTP API is instead configured directly on each `aws_apigatewayv2_route` via `authorization_type = "AWS_IAM"` with no authorizer resource. An implementer following the plan's 'IAM (SigV4) authorizer' framing who creates an `aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer` with `authorizer_type = "AWS_IAM"` will receive a Terraform/provider error; alternatively, an implementer who creates an authorizer at API level but omits `authorization_type` on individual routes may silently leave routes open to unsigned requests.
findings.location: What section — 'an API Gateway HTTP API with an IAM (SigV4) authorizer'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'an IAM (SigV4) authorizer' with: 'each `aws_apigatewayv2_route` carries `authorization_type = "AWS_IAM"` (the correct HTTP API v2 mechanism — there is no separate aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer resource for AWS_IAM on HTTP APIs)'; add an AC line asserting that a test verifies authorization_type = AWS_IAM on every declared route.

findings.finding: The plan uses the phrase 'IAM (SigV4) authorizer' — language that maps to REST API v1 semantics, where a named `aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer` resource with `authorizer_type = "AWS_IAM"` could be expected. In API Gateway HTTP API (v2), AWS_IAM auth is set per-route via `authorization_type = "AWS_IAM"` on `aws_apigatewayv2_route`; there is no separate `aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer` resource for AWS_IAM — only JWT and REQUEST/LAMBDA authorizer types exist for HTTP APIs.
findings.location: What section — 'an API Gateway HTTP API with an IAM (SigV4) authorizer'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'IAM (SigV4) authorizer' with 'AWS_IAM authorization set per-route (`authorization_type = "AWS_IAM"` on each `aws_apigatewayv2_route`)' to prevent the v1 authorizer-resource confusion.

findings.finding: The plan uses the phrase 'an IAM (SigV4) authorizer' for the API Gateway HTTP API, which echoes the REST API v1 concept of a named `aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer` resource. For HTTP APIs (APIGatewayV2), AWS_IAM auth is NOT a named authorizer resource — `aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer` only accepts `authorizer_type = "JWT"` or `"REQUEST"` (Lambda) for HTTP APIs. AWS_IAM is configured via `authorization_type = "AWS_IAM"` set on each `aws_apigatewayv2_route` individually. An implementer following the plan's 'authorizer' framing would create an `aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer` with `authorizer_type = "AWS_IAM"`, which Terraform will reject at validate/apply time; or they would apply AWS_IAM at the API level rather than per-route, leaving routes open.
findings.location: What section — 'an API Gateway HTTP API with an IAM (SigV4) authorizer, invoke permission over TLS'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, change 'an API Gateway HTTP API with an IAM (SigV4) authorizer' to 'an API Gateway HTTP API (APIGatewayV2) with `authorization_type = "AWS_IAM"` on every `aws_apigatewayv2_route` for the `/opcert/` paths — no separate `aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer` resource (HTTP APIs only support JWT and Lambda REQUEST authorizer types; AWS_IAM is route-level only).'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
